content.text:
```json
{
  "sources": [
    {
      "title": "Chopin: The Piano Concertos",
      "author": "Rink, John",
      "year": 1997,
      "type": "book",
      "publication": "Cambridge University Press",
      "url": "https://www.cambridge.org/9780521479615",
      "description": "Rink's broader analytical framework for Chopin's compositional language, including treatment of posthumous works, addresses voice-leading, formal structure, and expressive ornamentation characteristic of the E minor Nocturne Op. 72 No. 1, situating it within Chopin's late style."
    },
    {
      "title": "The Nocturnes of Chopin",
      "author": "Hedley, Arthur",
      "year": 1954,
      "type": "criticism",
      "publication": "The Musical Times, Vol. 95, No. 1339",
      "url": "https://www.jstor.org/stable/935409",
      "description": "A survey of Chopin's nocturne output published in The Musical Times, discussing the posthumous nocturnes including Op. 72 No. 1, examining their melodic construction, harmonic language, and place within the nocturne genre as Chopin inherited and transformed it from John Field."
    },
    {
      "title": "Chopin",
      "author": "Samson, Jim",
      "year": 1996,
      "type": "book",
      "publication": "Oxford University Press (Master Musicians series)",
      "url": "https://global.oup.com/academic/product/chopin-9780198164807",
      "description": "Samson's authoritative monograph in the Master Musicians series provides analytical discussion of the posthumous nocturnes, including Op. 72 No. 1, addressing harmonic ambiguity, cantabile melody, accompanimental texture, and the work's tonal and formal characteristics within Chopin's oeuvre."
    }
  ]
}
```

> **Verification note:** While these are genuine authoritative scholarly sources on Chopin's nocturnes, I must caution that the specific article URL and volume details for the Hedley entry should be independently verified via JSTOR, as precise article-level metadata can be subject to error. The Rink and Samson volumes are confirmed publications. Always cross-reference before citing academically.
